items.q: What is the function of the bridge in a pop song?
items.correct: To provide contrast before the final chorus, often by changing melody, key, or texture.
items.misconception: It is the same as the chorus.
items.random_wrong: It is the very first section.
items.why: Students confuse bridge with chorus because both are 'big' sections. The bridge appears once (usually after the second chorus) to provide CONTRAST and renew interest before returning.

items.q: What does the time signature 6/8 indicate?
items.correct: Two beats per bar, each beat divided into three (compound duple).
items.misconception: Six beats per bar, all equal in stress.
items.why: Students count 6/8 as six equal beats. In practice it's felt as TWO dotted-crotchet beats subdivided into three quavers each - compound duple, not simple sextuple.

items.q: How many crotchets are in a semibreve?
items.why: Students confuse the relative durations. A semibreve (whole note) = 2 minims = 4 crotchets = 8 quavers. Each level halves in length.
The answer examiners want is: John Williams. “Hans Zimmer” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Music heard only by audience is?
items.why: Correct answer: Non-diegetic. “Diegetic” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The real figure is 1977. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
